questions.scenario:
The monthly finance export service has this recovery target:

```text
RTO: next business day
RPO: last successful nightly export is acceptable
traffic: internal only
current preparation: backups, infrastructure code, deployment artifacts, and a restore runbook
standby compute: none
```

Which recovery strategy best fits this target?

options.text: Backup and restore, because the target tolerates a slower rebuild and low steady cost matters.
Multi-site active/active, because every workload should serve traffic from multiple Regions.
Warm standby, because next-business-day recovery requires a full duplicate stack running.
No recovery strategy, because internal services do not need restore evidence.

questions.explanation: Backup and restore can fit a workload with loose RTO/RPO targets when backups, infrastructure code, deployment artifacts, and a tested runbook exist. Paying for always-on standby capacity would not be justified by this target.

questions.scenario:
Checkout has this proposed recovery shape:

```text
RTO: 30 minutes
RPO: minutes
recovery Region: VPC, secrets, container images, task definitions, alarms, and replicated data are ready
ECS service: desired count is 0 until recovery
workers: scaled from 0 during recovery
traffic: shifted after validation
```

Which strategy is this closest to?

options.text: Pilot light, because the core path is ready while app compute starts and scales during recovery.
Backup and restore, because no recovery pieces exist until the incident.
Warm standby, because a small full stack is already handling reduced traffic.
Multi-site active/active, because both Regions are already serving customer writes.

questions.explanation: This is pilot light: important data and core infrastructure are ready, but application compute is switched off or scaled to zero until recovery. The RTO must include scaling, validation, and traffic movement.

questions.scenario:
The customer API has this recovery design:

```text
RTO: minutes
RPO: seconds to minutes
recovery Region: complete service stack already running at small capacity
normal state: health checks and synthetic traffic pass continuously
recovery action: scale up capacity and shift traffic
```

Which strategy is this closest to?

options.text: Warm standby, because a smaller but complete service is already running and can be scaled up.
Pilot light, because no application stack can process requests before recovery.
Backup and restore, because the team only keeps backups.
Archive tiering, because warm standby is a storage class.

questions.explanation: Warm standby keeps a scaled-down but functional copy of the workload running. It costs more than pilot light because more of the stack is alive before failure, but recovery has less work left.

questions.scenario:
A global payment path has this design:

```text
RTO: near zero for regional failure
RPO: near zero for normal regional failure
traffic: users are already served from more than one Region
data: application must handle cross-Region consistency and conflict rules
operations: deployments, alarms, and drills cover both Regions
```

Which strategy is this closest to?

options.text: Multi-site active/active, because multiple Regions already serve real traffic before failure.
Backup and restore, because near-zero RTO requires only snapshots.
Pilot light, because the application compute is switched off until recovery.
Single-AZ restore, because regional failure is handled by one Availability Zone.

questions.explanation: Multi-site active/active keeps multiple Regions serving traffic before a failure. It can reduce recovery time for a regional outage, but it is the most complex strategy because data consistency, traffic routing, deployments, and operations all become multi-Region concerns.

questions.scenario: Which statements are healthy strategy-selection habits?

options.text: Choose the cheapest tested strategy that can meet the business RTO and RPO.
Remember that faster recovery usually means more resources, replication, automation, and drills are ready before failure.
Keep point-in-time recovery options even when replication exists, because replication can copy bad writes.
Treat active/active as automatically simpler and cheaper than backup and restore.

questions.explanation: Strategy choice is a cost and evidence decision. More aggressive targets usually require more steady preparation. Replication and active/active designs help with availability, but bad writes still need point-in-time recovery and tested repair paths.
